a_victim.address_of_facility_include_name_and_not: Sunrise Oakview Assisted Living, 2145 Oakview Terrace, Apt 204, Sacramento, CA 95821

c_reporting_party.relation_to_victim_how_knows_of_abuse:
I am the evening-shift LVN at Sunrise Oakview Assisted Living and have been directly involved in providing medication administration and basic nursing care to Ms. Margaret Henderson since she moved into the facility in February 2024. I see her at least five days a week during my 2:00 PM–10:30 PM shift. Over the past six months I have had numerous one-on-one conversations with her during medication passes, vital sign checks, and while assisting with minor wound care for a skin tear on her forearm. During these interactions, Ms. Henderson has repeatedly expressed fear and anxiety specifically related to her son, Daniel Henderson, who is her primary financial power of attorney and who visits the facility approximately once every 1–2 weeks.

I first became concerned in late August 2024 when Ms. Henderson mentioned that she had "no money" to purchase new shoes and a replacement walker basket, even though I knew from our social worker and admission paperwork that she receives a monthly pension and Social Security benefits that should more than cover such needs. I also observed Daniel speaking to her in a raised voice on multiple occasions in the facility’s courtyard and in her room, calling her "useless" and "a burden" and telling her that she "owes" him for taking care of her finances. On at least two occasions (9/05/2024 and 10/12/2024), I was close enough to the room to hear him say things like, "If you keep making trouble and talking to staff about money, I will move you to a cheaper place where no one will bother to check on you," which I interpreted as a threat of abandonment or placement in a lower-quality facility.

I became aware of possible financial abuse when Ms. Henderson showed me an overdue notice for a podiatry bill on 11/02/2024, which indicated that her co-pay had not been paid for over 90 days. She told me that Daniel "takes my bank card" and only gives her "twenty dollars every few weeks" and that he has refused her requests for hearing aid batteries, new clothing, and to have her eyeglasses replaced. She stated that she had signed some papers last year giving him control of her accounts after he told her she was "too forgetful" to manage her own money. She seemed confused about what exactly she had signed but was very clear that she did not want him to "keep taking my money" and that she was afraid he would "be angry" if he learned she had told staff.

My knowledge of the abuse is based on: (1) multiple direct statements from the victim over a period of several months; (2) my own observations of Daniel’s verbally abusive and intimidating behavior during visits; (3) facility documentation and unpaid medical bills that are inconsistent with the level of income described in her admission paperwork; and (4) corroborating concerns raised informally by the facility social worker and two other caregivers about Daniel’s controlling behavior and refusal to authorize basic purchases for Ms. Henderson’s care and comfort.

e_reported_types_of_abuse.abuse_resulted_in_other_specify: Chronic emotional distress, increased anxiety around visits, and inability to obtain needed personal items (proper footwear, eyeglasses, clothing) and some recommended medical services due to financial control by the suspected abuser.

f_reporters_observations.reporters_observations_beliefs_statement:
Victim background and functional status:
Ms. Margaret L. Henderson is an 82-year-old widowed female who has resided at Sunrise Oakview Assisted Living since 2024-02-10. She has a history of hypertension, osteoarthritis in both knees, and mild cognitive impairment (short-term memory deficits, but generally oriented to person, place, and time when calm). She ambulates with a rolling walker and requires standby assistance for showers and some activities of daily living but is generally able to communicate her needs clearly. She appears soft-spoken, polite, and somewhat anxious, especially on days when she anticipates visits from her son, Daniel.

Nature of suspected financial abuse:
Over approximately the past 6–9 months, I have observed and documented several instances suggesting that Ms. Henderson’s son, Daniel Henderson (age 52), is misusing his authority as her financial power of attorney to control her access to funds and deprive her of necessary goods and services. Ms. Henderson has repeatedly told me that she does not have access to her own bank card or checkbook. She states that Daniel “took over everything” after she had a minor fall in late 2023 while still living in her own home. She recalls signing “some papers” at his urging but is unclear on the details; she believes these papers gave him full control of her finances.

Specific examples:
1. On 2024-08-22, Ms. Henderson asked me if the facility could “front her” money for a new pair of orthopedic shoes because her current shoes had worn soles and were causing her to shuffle and trip. When I suggested she ask her son, she became visibly tense, wrung her hands, and said, “He’ll just say no again. He says I don’t need anything and that I’m wasting his inheritance.” She reported that Daniel had refused to purchase new shoes for several months despite her repeated requests.

2. On 2024-09-05 at approximately 16:00, I passed by Ms. Henderson’s partially open door and overheard Daniel speaking to her in a harsh tone. He said, “Mom, you don’t understand how expensive this place is. You don’t need new glasses or fancy shoes. You should be thankful you’re not in one of those state homes.” Ms. Henderson was sitting on the edge of her bed, looking down at her hands. When I entered to do the scheduled 16:00 medication pass, she appeared tearful. After Daniel left, she told me quietly, “He gets so angry when I ask him about my money. I’m scared he’ll move me somewhere awful if I keep pushing.”

3. On 2024-11-02, Ms. Henderson showed me a letter she had received from a podiatry clinic indicating an outstanding balance of $186.40 for a visit that took place in July 2024. The letter was marked “FINAL NOTICE – ACCOUNT PAST DUE 90+ DAYS.” Ms. Henderson told me that Daniel had taken all mail related to her finances and that he “doesn’t like it” when she opens mail without him. She said she had hidden this particular letter from him because she was worried “they’ll send it to collections.” She also reported that Daniel had told her she “doesn’t need all these doctor visits” and that he was “not paying for unnecessary things.”

4. Between September and November 2024, facility staff (including myself and two caregivers) made multiple attempts to contact Daniel to obtain authorization for replacement eyeglasses after Ms. Henderson’s lenses became noticeably scratched, and she complained of difficulty reading and watching television. According to our records, Daniel either did not return calls or responded that “now is not a good time financially” and that “she can wait until after the holidays.” As of the date of this report (2025-12-10), Ms. Henderson still has not received new glasses, despite repeated recommendations.

5. Ms. Henderson has also complained that she receives only very small amounts of personal spending money. She stated to me on 2025-10-18, “He gives me twenty dollars, maybe once a month, and tells me not to bother him for more. I used to buy my own little things, like lotion and yarn, but now I have to ask staff to help me get the basics.” On at least two occasions, I have personally purchased small toiletry items (unscented lotion and lip balm) for her out of my own pocket because she said she had no cash and did not want to “make Daniel mad” by asking him.

Nature of emotional/psychological abuse and isolation:
In addition to the financial concerns, I have observed a pattern of verbal intimidation and emotional manipulation by Daniel during his visits. He typically visits every 1–2 weeks, usually on Friday afternoons around 15:00–17:00. During these visits, he often closes the door to Ms. Henderson’s room and speaks to her in a raised or harsh tone. On multiple occasions, staff have reported hearing him say things like:
- “If you keep telling the nurses about our business, I’ll move you somewhere cheaper and you won’t like it there.”
- “You’re lucky I haven’t just sold the house and put you in a county place.”
- “Stop complaining. You’re old; you don’t need all this stuff.”

Ms. Henderson consistently appears more anxious before his visits (pacing slightly with her walker, asking what time it is, and asking staff if we will be nearby). After he leaves, she often appears tearful or withdrawn. She has told me more than once, “Please don’t say anything to him. I don’t want him to get angry and send me away.” She has also said, “He tells me not to bother the staff and not to talk about money or he’ll cut me off.”

On at least three occasions (most recently on 2025-11-28 around 15:30), Daniel has told staff at the nurses’ station not to “interfere” in “family matters” and has asked that we “not encourage her to spend money on nonsense.” He has also attempted to limit her phone calls by telling her he will “take away the phone” if she calls other relatives to ask for help. Ms. Henderson reported that he disconnected her old landline when she lived at home because he said “scammers keep calling.” She now relies on the facility’s common-area phone and her room phone, which he sometimes unplugs when visiting, according to her statements.

Constraint/deprivation and neglect-related concerns:
While there is no evidence of physical assault or direct physical neglect by Daniel inside the facility (staff provide daily care), his financial control appears to be resulting in deprivation of necessary goods and services. Examples include:
- Delayed or denied medical co-pays (podiatry bill not paid, delaying follow-up care).
- Refusal or delay in purchasing replacement eyeglasses despite clear need.
- Refusal to authorize purchase of recommended orthopedic footwear and a more stable walker with a seat, which the physical therapist suggested after a near-fall incident in October 2024.
- Limiting funds for clothing; Ms. Henderson has mentioned that she has “only two pairs of comfortable pants” and “one decent sweater,” and she expressed embarrassment about wearing the same clothing repeatedly.

Mental and emotional impact on the victim:
Ms. Henderson displays signs of chronic anxiety, particularly around financial matters and her son’s visits. She often apologizes excessively when requesting basic items (like extra incontinence pads or a snack) and states things like, “I don’t want to be a burden” and “Daniel says I cost too much already.” She has difficulty sleeping on nights following his visits and has reported nightmares about being “sent away” to a “bad home.”

During a private conversation on 2025-11-29 at approximately 19:45, Ms. Henderson told me, “I’m afraid to say anything because he controls everything. If he gets mad, I’ll have nowhere to go.” When I asked if she wanted help, she nodded and said, “I just want to be able to buy what I need and not be scared all the time.” She explicitly gave verbal consent for me to share her concerns with Adult Protective Services, stating, “Please tell someone. Just don’t let him know I told you.”

Time frame and escalation:
The concerning behavior appears to have started at least by mid-2024, based on Ms. Henderson’s recollections, but has become more obvious and frequent since approximately August 2024. The most recent specific incident that prompted this formal report occurred on 2025-11-28 around 15:30. On that date, I walked past Ms. Henderson’s room and heard Daniel say, in a raised voice, “If you ask the staff for money again or tell them about our finances, I will move you out of here next month. I’m not joking.” Ms. Henderson responded in a shaky voice, “Please don’t, Danny. I’ll be good.” When I entered the room a few minutes later under the pretense of checking her blood pressure, Daniel glared at me and said, “We’re fine here. She’s just being dramatic,” and then quickly changed the subject.

Potential danger to investigator or staff:
At this time, I do not believe there is an immediate physical danger to investigators or staff. However, Daniel has shown a confrontational attitude toward staff when he feels questioned. He has raised his voice at the front desk staff and at me on one occasion, saying that we are “overstepping” and that “this is my mother and my money.” He appears easily angered when finances are discussed and may become verbally aggressive if he learns that APS or law enforcement has been contacted. I recommend that any investigator introduce themselves in a neutral, professional manner and, if possible, speak with Ms. Henderson privately without Daniel present.

My beliefs and reasons for reporting:
Based on repeated direct disclosures from the victim, my own observations of Daniel’s behavior and statements, facility documentation of unpaid medical bills and delayed purchases, and the pattern of fear and anxiety exhibited by Ms. Henderson, I strongly believe that she is being subjected to ongoing financial exploitation and emotional/psychological abuse by her son, who is in a position of trust and authority over her finances. I am concerned that, without intervention, she will continue to be deprived of necessary goods and services, and that her mental health will further deteriorate due to chronic fear and intimidation. I am also concerned about the possibility that Daniel may attempt to move her to a lower-quality or unsafe placement in retaliation for any perceived “complaints.”

I am submitting this report in good faith, with the victim’s verbal consent, and I am willing to provide additional details, copies of relevant bills/letters (through facility administration), and any further observations to assist in the investigation.

h_other_person_with_knowledge_of_abuse.address_other_person_with_knowledge: Sunrise Oakview Assisted Living, 2145 Oakview Terrace, Sacramento, CA 95821
h_other_person_with_knowledge_of_abuse.relationship_other_person_with_knowledge: Facility social worker assigned to Ms. Henderson; has reviewed financial documents in the admission packet and has also observed interactions between Ms. Henderson and her son and noted unpaid medical bills and delayed purchases.

i_family_member_or_responsible_person.relationship_family_or_responsible_perso: Son; holds financial power of attorney and is listed as primary responsible party for billing and financial decisions.
